title: Learning Diary
text:
About
Hi, welcome to my Learning Diary for Remotely Sensing City and Environments module! This module is part of the Bartlett Centre for Advanced Spatial Analysis (CASA), University College London (UCL) master’s level course in Urban Spatial Science.
My name is Hania and I am aspiring urban spatial scientist, but my background is in physics and arts. I am passionate about data visualisation and experimenting with different ways to communicate spatial analysis topics to broader audience.
Enjoy earth observation insights, remote sensing techniques and some infographics!
crumbs: About

title: 1  Introduction to Remote Sensing
text:
1.1 Spectral Signature in Research
This week we explored the fundamentals of Earth Observation (EO), which is the science of collecting and analysing date about our planet from a distance.
The core of this process is Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR). As Figure 1.2 show, EO satellites don’t just take a photo; they capture how EMR interacts with Earth’s surface through absorption, transmission, or scattering. I found the distinction between sensor types useful for understanding data constraints:
For me, highlight form this lecture was to learn that everything on Earth has a unique spectral signature-a digital fingerprint based on how it reflects light across different wavelengths. While the concept is straightforward, the complexity arises when trying to distinguish between similar surfaces, especially when atmospheric interference modify the signal.
To better grasp spectral signatures we will look at environmental monitoring example. Pivovar et al. (2023) demonstrate how hyperspectral imaging can detect stress in Norway spruce trees before the damage is even visible to the human eye. By analyzing subtle shifts in the trees spectral signatures under thermal stress, researchers can identify physiological changes early. This is exactly what I find so fascinating about it! It shows that EO can be more than “just” a mapping tool and can act as a diagnostic tool for forest health.
Similarly, these signatures allow us to create indices like NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index), which quantifies greenness by exploiting the contrast between visible red and near-infrared reflectance. This brings us to Alex de la Iglesia Martinez (2022), who discusses the link between RS and polices, by applying NDVI to urban policy. Their work demystifies how these calculations can inform greening interventions in cities.
Nevertheless, both of these approaches come with limitations. NDVI, although simple and widely used, is often seasonally dependent and can be inaccurate in areas with sparse vegetation or high soil background (more on that in Correction for Remote Sensing chapter!). On the other hand, the hyperspectral approach used by Pivovar et al. (2023) requires a lot of computational power and specialised sensors that aren’t as readily available as standard multispectral satellites. This makes it difficult to scale their high-detail diagnostic method to a global or even regional level compared to the more accessible NDVI.
crumbs: 1  Introduction to Remote Sensing

title: 1  Introduction to Remote Sensing
text:
1.2 Reflections




Alex de la Iglesia Martinez, S. M. Labib. 2022. “Demystifying Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) for Greenness Exposure Assessments and Policy Interventions in Urban Greening.” Environmental Research 220. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2022.115155.


Pivovar, Matúš, Roope Näsi, Eija Honkavaara, Miroslav Blaženec, Jaroslav Škvarenina, Roman Modlinger, Jaroslav Rožnovský, and Rastislav Jakuš. 2023. “Spectral Signatures Discrimination of Norway Spruce Trees Under Experimentally Induced Drought and Acute Thermal Stress Using Hyperspectral Imaging.” ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing 196: 282–301.
crumbs: 1  Introduction to Remote Sensing

title: 2  Week 2
text: Here are the slides for Week 2:
crumbs: 2  Week 2

title: 3  Corrections for Remote Sensing
text:
3.1 Filtering, Texture Analysis and Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
When dealing with satellite data, in most cases, we will deal with pre-processed imagery, which is ready to analyse. There is number of remote sensing data corrections and in this section I would like to introduce few of them, with a main focus on specific ratio index - Normalised Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI).
Before focusing on NDVI, I explored several image processing techniques introduced in the practical, including filtering, texture analysis and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Visually, this did not necessarily “improve” the image, but it reduces small-scale pixel noise and stabilises local variation. This is particularly relevant when performing further statistical operations (e.g. texture or PCA), as these methods can otherwise be overly sensitive to abrupt pixel-to-pixel differences that may reflect sensor noise rather than real landscape structure. I then calculated GLCM texture (homogeneity) using a 7×7 window. Unlike spectral bands, texture does not measure reflectance but rather the spatial arrangement of pixel values. High homogeneity indicates uniform surfaces (e.g. large vegetated patches or water bodies), while lower values suggest more heterogeneous urban fabric. However, texture outputs are less intuitive to interpret visually and highly dependent on chosen parameters (window size and grey level quantisation). Finally, PCA was applied to the stacked spectral and texture layers to reduce redundancy between correlated bands. PCA transforms the data into new components (PC1, PC2, etc.) that capture decreasing proportions of overall variance. While PCA can be useful for dimensionality reduction and classification, the resulting components are abstract combinations of original variables and therefore less directly interpretable than NDVI. Overall, these steps demonstrated how preprocessing choices influence both visual outputs and statistical stability, even if their interpretability is less straightforward than vegetation indices.
crumbs: 3  Corrections for Remote Sensing

title: 3  Corrections for Remote Sensing
text:
3.2 The Normalised Different Vegetation Index (NDVI)








London NDVI Map







Warsaw NDVI Map






Figure 3.1: Side-by-side spatial analysis of vegetation index. Click either image to expand and compare scales.



My initial spin on practical content came from genuine interest in comparison of green coverage between London and Warsaw. I started off with purely looking at the visualisation of NDVI for both cities wondering if there are significant differences visible to the naked eye. For both cities I used imagery from May 2024, to minimise differences which might be caused by seasonal variations. The minimum of NDVI value was chosen based on fact that for urban areas NDVI > 0.2 is considered to be threshold for “some” vegetation. Maximum NDVI was set to be 0.6, as for London NDVI > 0.6 is less than 0.001% of the city area, and for Warsaw max NDVI value is 0.58.
Although some differences are visible, visualisations weren’t very informative. I decided to run very basic descriptive statistics (see Table 3.1), which revealed a bit more about cities greenery. Warsaw had a slightly higher percentage of vegetation cover, while London seems to have higher value for max NDVI (as well as slightly higher mean NDVI) indicating spots of more lush greenery in the city. The vegetation cover of 66% and 64% seemed like very high values, and after cross-checking with literature it turned to be way off. According to Mayor of London website London green cover is around 51.7%, while Husqvarna Urban Green Space Insights (HUGSI) website shows that London green space is 41%, and Warsaw 50%.
This variations might be partially due to differences in definitions of green space (maybe different thresholds or not accounting for private gardens or green roofs) , but mostly due to simplicity of the method I used. Firstly Landsat 8 data has resolution of 30 m, while Sentinel 2 (used in HUGSI analysis) has a resolution of 10-20 m. When using Landsat 8 data we are analysing 900 m² pixels, which can lead to eg. having very small, but very healthy green backyard which can push an average NDVI pixel value to be > 0.2, and therefore count the whole pixel as a vegetation.



Table 3.1: NDVI Comparison: Warsaw vs. London





City
Max NDVI
Mean NDVI (Veg)
Veg Cover %




Warsaw

title: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study
text:
4.1 Making Cities Resilient 2030 (MCR2030)
MCR 2030 is an initiative which aims to strengthen local resilience of the cities around the world. It was launched by United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) in collaboration with partners like Global Resilient Cities Network, ICLEI, C40 Cities Climate Leadership and many others (for full list please see this document), after a success of 10-year (2010-2020) campaign “Making Cities Resilient”. Key goals of this partnership is to help cities transition from raising awareness on local resilience to actually implementing risk-reduction and resilient infrastructure. It helps cities align with Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction, the Paris Agreement, and the Sustainable Development Goals (specifically SDG11).
crumbs: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study

title: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study
text:
4.1.1 Wroclaw as a Resilience Hub
As a case study I decided to choose my hometown- Wroclaw. It’s 4 largest city in Poland, located at southwestern part of the country, on the Odra (Oder) river. It’s location makes it prone to the flooding, and city has experience few of them especially in past 50 years. One of the biggest disasters happened in 1997, when Oder and Morava rivers caused flooding in Central Europe. After 1997 flood Wroclaw’s authorities focused on city’s risk-reduction and Wroclaw has become MCR Resilience Hub in 2022, leading an example of resilient infrastructure development. While preparing material for this entry I realised that, because Wroclaw has a solid experience in improving its infrastructure, it’s local authorities are already using remote sensing and spatial analysis in implementing those changes. In case of flooding there is also a well-functioning system in place - Copernicus Emergency Management Service (CEMS), which provides on demand mapping in case of natural hazards.






Figure 4.1: On Demand Mapping on CEMS website- case of floods in South West Poland in 2024.



I started thinking maybe there is no much space for suggestions how to use earth observation in supporting MCR2030-Wroclaw seems to be greener and more resilient to flooding. However, resilience does not stop at administrative borders, and a city’s safety is often dictated by the land-use choices of its immediate neighbours.
crumbs: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study

title: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study
text:
4.2 Resilient Infrastructure in Suburban Areas
While Wroclaw strengthens its internal defenses, the rapid expansion of the surrounding functional urban area presents a new set of vulnerabilities. What about suburban areas which don’t fall under the boundaries of cities? In polish administrative system they are separate entities, with their own mayors, but often falls into bigger agglomeration (like in case of Wroclaw). Being in an agglomeration would mean some degree of collaboration between city president (in Poland cities has presidents, and municipalities has mayors) and mayors of surrounding municipalities, but there is no rigours rules regarding the urban development of city vs its suburban areas. This leads to the situations like Wroclaw case where there are years of efforts to re-green city, making it more resilient, but just outside its boundaries there is rapid urban sprawl happening, with developments built on potential flood zones and intense “soil sealing” with no supporting drainage systems. My suggestion would be not only regarding the use of RS in urban planning but also to create a stronger administrative body responsible of more sustainable (and probably more controlled) development of suburban areas.
crumbs: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study

title: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study
section: 4.3 Proposed Improvement: The Agglomeration Resilience Authority
text:
4.3 Proposed Improvement: The Agglomeration Resilience Authority
To address the disconnect between city planning and suburban sprawl, I suggest the establishment of an Agglomeration Resilience Authority. This would serve as a supra-municipal governing body empowered to oversee land-use permits across the entire metropolitan region. Its primary role would be to ensure that local suburban developments do not compromise regional flood safety or ecological health. By using Remote Sensing (RS) as its primary evidence-based tool, this authority could make informed, objective decisions in the following ways:

Standardizing “Soil Sealing” Limits: By using High-Resolution Multispectral Imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) to calculate the Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI), the authority can set strict “permeability quotas.” This prevents excessive concrete cover in the suburbs from increasing runoff and overwhelming Wroclaw’s drainage systems.
Dynamic Flood Risk Mapping: Utilizing LiDAR-derived Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) allows for the identification of natural drainage corridors and micro-depressions. The authority can use this data to veto “high-risk” developments in areas that RS identifies as natural water retention zones, even if they aren’t marked on outdated traditional maps.
Monitoring Trans-boundary Green Belts: Using the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), the body can ensure that “Green Lungs” remain continuous across administrative borders. RS allows for real-time tracking of whether suburban encroachment is fragmenting the forests and wetlands that the entire agglomeration relies on for cooling and water absorption.
crumbs: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study

title: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study
text:
4.4 Conclusion: Data-Driven Solidarity
By integrating Remote Sensing into a unified administrative framework, we move from “isolated resilience” to “territorial resilience.” This approach directly supports the MCR2030 goal of transitioning from awareness to implementation by ensuring urban growth is monitored through the lens of environmental physics rather than political boundaries. For Wroclaw to remain a true Resilience Hub, it must look beyond its city signs and use Earth Observation to lead its entire metropolitan region toward a safer, more porous future. This data-driven solidarity ensures that a win for one municipality is not a disaster for its neighbour.
crumbs: 4  Remoet Sensing and Policies- Case Study

title: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.1 What is Google Earth Engine (GEE)?
This week focuses on Google Earth Engine (GEE), a cloud-based platform designed for planetary-scale geospatial analysis. GEE represented a fundamental shift in our ability to monitor the Earth, and ten years later, we are exploring whether it lived up to the hype. In the following sections we will first set some background information what GEE is and what datasets we can find there, to later evaluate its real-world impact on the industry.
Because I’m completely new to GEE I would like to start with writing up some basic information about it. GEE is cloud-based platform which provides massive amount of geo-spatial data, with planetary-scale analysis tools. The main components of GEE, which makes it (in theory) so impactful are:
crumbs: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
The Public Data Archive : GEE contains ready-to-use 40 years historical and current data from sensors such as Sentinel, MODIS, or Landsat.
The Computational Infrastructure : it runs on Google servers, uses data stored in cloud (no downloading needed) which effects in extremely fast and efficient data analysis (unlike traditional GIS).
The APIs : users can interact with platform using JavaScript or Python scripts. No configuring hardware or managing backend infrastructure.
crumbs: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.2 GEE in real world
So what has shifted in geo-big data research after GEE was launched? As highlighted by Tamiminia et al. (2020) in their meta-analysis, GEE has fundamentally shifted the focus of remote sensing toward large-scale monitoring, with a heavy reliance on the Landsat archive and Random Forest classification algorithms (more on classification in next chapter!). This trend is mirrored in urban studies, such as those reviewed by Martinez-Arboleda, Morales-Hernandez, and Ordoñez-Galindo (2023), where GEE’s ability to process Land Surface Temperature (LST) data allows for the assessment of Urban Heat Islands (UHI) at a granularity previously impossible without high-performance local computing. In these contexts, GEE acts as a “democratizer” of data, allowing researchers to move from local case studies to national or even global assessments of nature-based solutions like urban green spaces.
However, a critical evaluation of the literature reveals significant methodological and systemic biases. Methodologically, there is a persistent dominance of pixel-based rather than object-based classification; while more efficient in a cloud environment, pixel-based approaches often ignore the spatial context and “texture” of the landscape, potentially leading to lower accuracy in complex urban environments. More broadly, the evidence base itself may be skewed. Most systematic reviews, including those by Tamiminia et al. (2020), primarily sample English-language journals indexed in databases like Scopus or Web of Science. This creates underrepresentation of research conducted in other languages or published in regional journals, making it difficult to assess if GEE’s “global impact” is truly representative of global needs or just reflective of the priorities of the English-speaking academic North.
crumbs: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.3 Reflections




Martinez-Arboleda, Joan Sebastian, Mario Morales-Hernandez, and Adriana Ordoñez-Galindo. 2023. “A Systematic Review of Remote Sensing and Geographic Information Systems Based Techniques for Monitoring and Assessing the Impact of Urban Green Spaces on Urban Heat Island.” Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment 29: 100897. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rsase.2022.100897.


Tamiminia, Haifa, Bahram Salehi, Masoud Mahdianpari, Ladan Beani, Brian Postrick, Brian Giammanco, and Brian Brisco. 2020. “Google Earth Engine for Geo-Big Data Applications: A Meta-Analysis and Systematic Review.” ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing 164: 152–70. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2020.04.001.
crumbs: 5  Introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice
text:
6.1 Machine Learning in Action: The Pixel Based approach
Note: This entry is combination of Classification I and Classification II lectures, as those lectures are closely related and it felt more natural that way!
Now that we’ve seen how GEE functions in the real world, it’s time to dive deeper into the specific analysis methods used to interpret satellite imagery. This entry focuses on classification - the process of using machine learning algorithms to categorize pixels into discrete land cover or land use classes (e.g., water, forest, or urban) based on their spectral characteristics. To help visualize the workflows used in remote sensing, I’ve mapped out the general process below: In the following sections, I’ll compare Pixel-Based Classification with Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA), critically reflecting on when to use each and the limitations they bring to a project.
As a simple example of pixel-based classification, using Random Forest (RF), I’ll use workflow demonstrated in the practical. Here is little snippet of practical showing the supervised classification using random forest of 100 decision trees:
This produced a Land Use/Land Cover (LULC) map of Wroclaw:
While the result looks ok, I’m quite skeptical of the training process. Because I manually selected the training polygons, the model is entirely dependent on my subjective visual assessment of what looks like “forest” versus “general vegetation” or “urban.” This manual approach doesn’t guarantee high accuracy. Furthermore, the output is notably “grainy” - a fundamental limitation of pixel-based methods.
crumbs: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice

title: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice
crumbs: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice

title: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice
text:
6.2 Shifting the Approach: Testing OBIA
To “smooth out” the LULC map and improve spatial logic, I decided to shift the workflow toward OBIA. Unlike pixel-based methods, OBIA groups pixels into meaningful “objects” (like an individual building or a lake) through a process called segmentation before classifying them.
My first attempt at OBIA was quite poor- it appeared “blocky” and unnatural, with results that felt less representative than my initial pixel-based map. I decided to tune in some of the SNIC segmentation parameters going from:
var snic = ee.Algorithms.Image.Segmentation.SNIC({
  image: waytwo_clip, 
  size: 20,
  compactness: 1,
  connectivity: 8,
  neighborhoodSize: 128
}).select(['B2_mean', 'B3_mean', 'B4_mean', 'B5_mean', 'B6_mean', 'B7_mean'], 
          ['B2', 'B3', 'B4', 'B5', 'B6', 'B7']); 
to:
var snic = ee.Algorithms.Image.Segmentation.SNIC({
  image: waytwo_clip, 
  size: 10,           // REDUCED: smaller seeds = finer detail for urban areas
  compactness: 0,     // SET TO 0: allows segments to be irregular/organic shapes 
  connectivity: 8,   
  neighborhoodSize: 256 // INCREASED: gives the algorithm more "room" to find boundaries
}).select(['B2_mean', 'B3_mean', 'B4_mean', 'B5_mean', 'B6_mean', 'B7_mean'], 
          ['B2', 'B3', 'B4', 'B5', 'B6', 'B7']);
Although spatial coherence improved after those adjustments, the overall thematic accuracy still looked worse than my first map (see Figure 6.3).






Figure 6.2: Visualising making of LULC map of Wroclaw, using OBIA method.
crumbs: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice

title: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice
section: 6.3 Refining the “Object”- Why My OBIA Failed
text:
6.3 Refining the “Object”- Why My OBIA Failed
Through this process, I realised that for OBIA to be truly effective, the Random Forest algorithm needs more “descriptors” than a pixel-based model. In my trial, I only provided spectral means (the average colour of the object). To improve this, I would need to perform more detailed feature extraction between the segmentation and classification stages (not limited only to mean spectral values). By extracting textural and geometric descriptors, I could provide the Random Forest with the context it needs to distinguish between spectrally similar classes, for instance, telling the difference between a smooth grass lawn and a rough forest canopy based on texture rather than just greenness.
As noted by Shafizadeh-Moghadam et al. (2021), sophisticated models often incorporate GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix) texture measures and geometric attributes, such as “linearity” for identifying roads. This transforms the input from a simple “mean colour” into a multi-dimensional description of what a feature looks like and how it is shaped-an essential step for mapping complex urban environments like Wroclaw. Here is great depiction of such complex OBIA workflow:






Figure 6.3: Flowchart of the general process of LULC classification within the GEE environment.- Shafizadeh-Moghadam et al. (2021)




6.3.1 …so what is the best approach?
While well-performed OBIA offers very detailed, and context-sensitive maps, it does come with at the cost- it’s more computationally expensive and requires more expertise than simple pixel-based RF classification. Due to those limitations it would be difficult to work with it when we want to map global changes like Global Forest Change. Hansen et al. (2013) mapped global forest change, using 30m pixel resolution classification. It’s great for monitoring the big picture, but as mentioned here it comes with serious shortcomings. For instance, some deforested areas converted into plantations might be still be classified as a forests.
In cases where classification based on a whole 30m pixel fails, sub-pixel analysis offers a solution. Chen et al. (2021) demonstrated this by using Landsat time series to monitor forest degradation. Unlike Hansen’s binary approach (forest vs. non-forest), sub-pixel analysis uses Spectral Mixture Analysis to estimate the fraction of vegetation within a pixel. This allows detection subtle changes (like selective logging) where the canopy is thinned but the pixel hasn’t technically turned into a field yet.
All those technical subtleties loops back to treating science as an art, and knowing that at the end it is subjective, and might come with (un)intentional biases. What works for global mapping might often fail for the local scale, and interpreting results obtained from classification, should involve checking them against other types of evidence.
crumbs: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice

title: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice
text:
6.4 Reflections
These two weeks were super interesting, though conceptually quite a stretch! Since I’m also taking CASA0006, it was great to see a bridge forming between ML theory and remote sensing practice. It served as a solid revision of what we’ve covered in Data Science module, but from a different angle. Seeing the tangible results you can achieve with spatial data makes the complexity of ML feel much more exciting.
I still struggle to navigate different classification workflows, and I know that learning to adjust parameters with confidence will take time and practice. Nevertheless, I really enjoyed getting into the weeds of classification and I’m looking forward to gaining more fluency in combined ML and RS projects. In the future, I would love to be able to write algorithms for detecting urban growth or identifying informal settlements.




Chen, Shijuan, Curtis E Woodcock, Eric L Bullock, Paulo Arévalo, Paata Torchinava, Siqi Peng, and Pontus Olofsson. 2021. “Monitoring Temperate Forest Degradation on Google Earth Engine Using Landsat Time Series Analysis.” Remote Sensing of Environment 265: 112648. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2021.112648.


Hansen, Matthew C, Peter V Potapov, Rebecca Moore, Matt Hancher, Svetlana A Turubanova, Alexandra Tyukavina, David Thau, et al. 2013. “High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change.” Science 342 (6160): 850–53. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1244693.


Shafizadeh-Moghadam, Hossein, Morteza Khazaei, Seyed Kazem Alavipanah, and Qihao Weng. 2021. “Google Earth Engine for Large-Scale Land Use and Land Cover Mapping: An Object-Based Classification Approach Using Spectral, Textural and Topographical Factors.” GIScience & Remote Sensing 58 (6): 914–28. https://doi.org/10.1080/15481603.2021.1947623.
crumbs: 6  Machine Learning in Remote Sensing Practice

text:
